purpose: Draft (inactive) question objects derived from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch -- a completed, handwritten 'Review Quiz #1' (20 fill-in-blank questions) plus a liferaft parts-labeling worksheet. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted, after each question's verificationStatus is upgraded from draft-unverified and each fact is checked against Vinci's confirmed manual copy (per the source intake's needsVerification/verificationStatus fields, which are unverified on every source item).
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless: (1) its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified' following direct confirmation against the manual, and (2) for the 5 image-based questions, the shared liferaft-diagram photo (2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg) has been cropped to remove the pencil doodles/scribbles overlaid on the raw photo -- see public/content/vinci/2026-07-08-quiz-review-1/README.md.
duplicateCheckNotes: Cross-referenced against all 145 live questions in src/content/course.json (not just chapter-2/chapter-3) before drafting. Skipped as clear duplicates: Q1 'What does the Station Bill list?' (duplicates q-ch1-vocab-stationbill-easy), Q3 'abandon ship drill passenger actions' (the 'report to muster station' half duplicates a choice already in q-ch1-abandonship-drill-components-medium), Q6 'limber holes' (duplicates q-gloss-limber), and Q10 'close the drain before launch' (duplicates q-ch2-boatplug / q-ch2-drainplugfirst-hard). Q16 ('Oars' command alone) was folded into the combined rowing-commands question (q-quizr1-rowing-commands-compare-vh) rather than drafted separately, since testing it standalone would have restated a fact already covered there. Chapter 3 was prioritized per instructions: of the 6 fresh liferaft facts drafted, all were deliberately chosen to AVOID the liferaft topics the live bank already covers heavily (righting a capsized raft / CO2-bottle-side technique -- tested 4 ways already by q-raft-1/2/7/11; ballast-bag purpose alone -- q-raft-9; canopy-alone benefit -- q-raft-5; inflation-trigger mechanism -- q-raft-3), instead drafting parts of the diagram with zero existing coverage (weak link, sea anchor, inflatable floor, internal/external lifelines, recognition light, water-activated battery, floating sheath knife). Diagram item #15 (left blank by the student, no visible numbered arrow) and the 'PRV' vs. 'Pressure pellet valve' label discrepancy on item #4 were both excluded entirely rather than guessed, per instructions.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT one of the survival items typically stowed in a lifeboat's equipment locker?

choices.text: A boathook
choices.misconception: This is real lifeboat equipment, not the odd one out.

choices.text: A folding camp stove
A bilge pump
choices.misconception: This is real lifeboat equipment, not the odd one out.

choices.text: An instruction card
choices.misconception: This is real lifeboat equipment, not the odd one out.

hints: The real list is long -- bailer, bilge pump, boathook, bucket, can opener, compass, dipper, drinking cup, fire extinguisher, first aid kit, fishing kit, flashlight, hatchet, heaving line, instruction card, jackknife, ladder, mirror.
One of these four items isn't on that list at all.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's survival equipment locker includes items like a bailer, bilge pump, boathook, bucket, can opener, compass, dipper, drinking cup, fire extinguisher, first aid kit, fishing kit, flashlight, hatchet, heaving line, instruction card, jackknife, ladder, and mirror -- but not a camp stove.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-2.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 2 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What purpose does the double-ended design of an open lifeboat serve?

choices.text: Being pointed at both bow and stern makes the boat more seaworthy -- less likely to be swamped or to 'broach to'
It lets the boat be rowed equally well in either direction, since bow and stern are otherwise identical
choices.misconception: Overstates symmetry-of-use rather than the actual seaworthiness rationale.

choices.text: It's purely a manufacturing cost-saving shape
choices.misconception: Attributes a safety design feature to a cost consideration with no supporting basis.

choices.text: It allows the boat to self-right if capsized
choices.misconception: Confuses the double-ended hull shape with the separate, unrelated self-righting feature.

hints: Think about what happens to a boat's stability in following seas or when waves hit from behind.
'Broach to' means the boat swings broadside to the waves -- a dangerous position in rough water.
questions.explanation: Open lifeboats are normally double-ended (pointed at both bow and stern), which makes them more seaworthy and less likely to be swamped or to 'broach to' (turn broadside to the waves).
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-2.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 4 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: How can crew members determine a lifeboat's personnel capacity?

choices.text: Check the nameplate, which lists the manufacturer's name, serial number, date of manufacture, light/full load figures, and the number of persons allowed; this figure may also be marked on the bow in permanent characters
Count the number of oars stowed aboard
choices.misconception: Oar count has no bearing on certified personnel capacity.

choices.text: Ask the ship's captain, since capacity isn't recorded anywhere on the boat itself
choices.misconception: Capacity IS recorded directly on the boat, via the nameplate and bow marking.

choices.text: Measure the boat's overall length only
choices.misconception: Length alone doesn't determine certified capacity -- that comes from the nameplate/marking.

hints: This information lives directly on the boat, not in separate paperwork.
One specific plate lists several pieces of information together: who made it, when, and how many people it's rated for.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's nameplate lists the manufacturer's name, serial number, date of manufacture, light-load and full-load figures, and the number of persons allowed -- this is how crew determine personnel capacity. The figure may also be marked on the bow in permanent characters.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-3.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 5 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Why should iron or steel tools not be left lying in or near an aluminum lifeboat?

choices.text: Possibility of electrolytic corrosion between the dissimilar metals
The tools would simply rust the aluminum through ordinary oxidation, regardless of what metal they're made of
choices.misconception: Describes ordinary rust, not the dissimilar-metal electrolytic reaction that's actually the concern.

choices.text: The extra weight could exceed the boat's rated capacity
choices.misconception: Unrelated to the actual corrosion risk -- a few hand tools don't meaningfully affect capacity.

choices.text: Steel tools would demagnetize the boat's compass
choices.misconception: Confuses a magnetic-interference concern with an unrelated corrosion concern.

hints: This is about a chemical reaction between two different metals in contact, not simple rust.
Aluminum is especially reactive when paired with a more "noble" metal like iron or steel in the presence of moisture.
questions.explanation: Aluminum lifeboats are especially subject to electrolytic corrosion if iron or steel tools are left on or near the boat -- the two dissimilar metals in contact (with moisture present) drive a corrosive reaction that eats into the aluminum.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-3.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 7 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: In a lifeboat, foot rests are also known by what alternate term, and what are they for?

choices.text: Stretchers -- provided for rowers to brace their feet against while rowing
Thwarts -- the seating benches rowers sit on
choices.misconception: Confuses foot rests with the separate seating structure (thwarts) rowers sit on.

choices.text: Gratings -- removable deck panels
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated fitting instead of the actual alternate term.

choices.text: Skids -- launch-rail fittings used during lowering
choices.misconception: Confuses foot rests with unrelated launch hardware.

hints: This term also shows up in a medical context (for carrying an injured person), but here it means something a rower's feet touch.
Rowers need something to push against with their feet to get power into each stroke.
questions.explanation: Foot rests in a lifeboat are also known as stretchers -- provided so rowers can brace their feet against them while rowing.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-3.jpg (Review Quiz #1, questions 8 and 11 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A lifeboat coxswain gives rowing commands in sequence. What does each of 'Oars', 'Backwater all', and 'Way enough' actually tell the rowers to do?

choices.text: 'Oars': put the oars horizontal to the water with blades feathered; 'Backwater all': place the oar blades in the water perpendicular to the surface with the handle against the chest, to reverse; 'Way enough': finish the stroke, lift the oars from the rowlocks, and stow them on the side bench with blades pointing forward
'Oars': stow the oars completely; 'Backwater all': row forward at full effort; 'Way enough': begin rowing
choices.misconception: Reassigns all three commands to actions they don't actually mean.

choices.text: All three commands mean the same thing -- stop rowing
choices.misconception: Collapses three distinct commands, each with a different specific rower action, into one.

choices.text: 'Way enough' is given before rowing begins, to signal readiness
choices.misconception: Reverses when 'way enough' is actually used -- it's a stop/finish command given at the end, not a starting signal.

hints: These three commands form a natural sequence: a ready/pause position, an active reversing action, and a full stop-and-stow action -- in that rough order of "how much is happening."
Notice which command leaves the oars still in the rowlocks versus stowed away entirely.
questions.explanation: 'Oars' means put the oars horizontal to the water with blades feathered (a ready/pause position). 'Backwater all' means place the oar blades in the water perpendicular to the surface with the handle against the chest, to drive the boat backward. 'Way enough' means finish the current stroke, lift the oars from the rowlocks, and stow them on the side bench with blades pointing forward -- the oars come in from the bow.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, images 2026-07-07_063330-4.jpg and 2026-07-07_063330-5.jpg (Review Quiz #1, questions 12, 15, and 16 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What signal is given to commence lowering the lifeboats?

choices.text: One short blast on the whistle
Seven or more short blasts followed by one long blast
choices.misconception: This is the general emergency alarm signal (meaning 'proceed to muster stations'), a different signal from the one that starts lowering.

choices.text: Three long blasts
choices.misconception: Invents an incorrect signal pattern.

choices.text: A continuous horn blast until boarding is complete
choices.misconception: Invents an incorrect signal.

hints: This is a short, simple signal -- distinct from the longer general emergency alarm pattern used to call people to muster stations in the first place.
questions.explanation: The signal to commence lowering the lifeboats is one short blast on the whistle -- a distinct, later-stage signal from the general emergency alarm (seven or more short blasts plus one long blast) that first calls everyone to muster stations.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-4.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 13 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: In open lifeboats, how is the releasing lever specifically labeled and marked?

choices.text: Painted red, labeled 'Danger -- lever drops boat', with a 12-inch-wide white stripe
Painted the same international orange as the rest of the boat, with no special labeling
choices.misconception: Misses that the lever itself gets distinct red/white marking, separate from the boat's general international-orange paint.

choices.text: Painted green with a 'Caution' label only
choices.misconception: Wrong color, and understates the warning wording actually used.

choices.text: Left unpainted, relying on a placard posted elsewhere on the boat
choices.misconception: Misses that the lever itself is directly painted and labeled, not just referenced by a separate placard.

hints: The lever's own color is a strong contrast to the boat's general orange scheme, on purpose.
The wording isn't a vague 'caution' -- it spells out exactly what pulling the lever does.
questions.explanation: In open lifeboats, the releasing lever is painted red and labeled 'Danger -- lever drops boat', with a 12-inch-wide white stripe -- a deliberately unmistakable marking distinct from the boat's general international-orange paint scheme.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-4.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 14 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the minimum speed requirement for a Class 1 motor lifeboat?

choices.text: At least 6 knots, in smooth water, when loaded with its full complement of persons and equipment
At least 6 knots, but only measured when the boat is empty
choices.misconception: The requirement is measured fully loaded, not empty -- an empty-boat test would overstate real performance.

choices.text: At least 12 knots, fully loaded
choices.misconception: Overstates the actual required speed.

choices.text: No minimum speed is specified, only that the engine must start reliably
choices.misconception: Drops the actual speed requirement entirely.

hints: The test condition matters as much as the number -- it has to be loaded the way it would actually be used.
This figure is measured in smooth water, not rough seas.
questions.explanation: A Class 1 motor lifeboat, in smooth water when loaded with its full complement of persons and equipment, must make at least 6 knots.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-5.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 17 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Who is responsible for taking a muster of all personnel assigned to a lifeboat?

choices.text: The coxswain
Each individual passenger, self-reporting as present
choices.misconception: Shifts responsibility away from the coxswain's actual role.

choices.text: The ship's captain personally, at every individual lifeboat
choices.misconception: Overstates the captain's direct role at the individual-boat level.

choices.text: No one specifically -- headcounts only happen after launch
choices.misconception: Incorrectly assumes no one is responsible for a muster before launch.

hints: This is the same person in charge of commanding the boat's launch and steering.
questions.explanation: The coxswain is responsible for taking a muster of all personnel assigned to their lifeboat.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-5.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 18 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What can be done on a lifeboat with a sail if the winds become too strong?

choices.text: Reef the sail (reduce the area of sail exposed to the wind)
Remove the mast entirely and switch to rowing, since sails have no adjustment option
choices.misconception: Skips the actual adjustment option (reefing) in favor of an unnecessary full removal.

choices.text: Raise a second, larger sail for more control
choices.misconception: Worsens the situation by increasing sail area instead of reducing it.

choices.text: Nothing can be done once the sail is set
choices.misconception: Incorrectly assumes no mid-course adjustment is possible.

hints: Think about reducing the amount of sail catching the wind, not removing the sail system entirely.
questions.explanation: If winds become too strong on a lifeboat under sail, the sail should be reefed -- reducing the area of sail exposed to the wind rather than removing the rig entirely.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-5.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 19 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What color is a lifeboat's sail?

choices.text: Indian orange
International orange, the same as the boat's canopy and hull markings
choices.misconception: Confuses Indian orange (used for sails and canvas canopies) with international orange (used for the boat's hull/canopy exterior and seating) -- two distinct terms for two different components.

choices.text: White, for maximum visibility against the water
choices.misconception: Invents an incorrect color.

choices.text: Whatever color the manufacturer had on hand -- sail color isn't regulated
choices.misconception: Incorrectly assumes sail color isn't a specified requirement.

hints: There are two similarly-named 'orange' terms in this course -- one for sails/canvas, one for hulls/canopies -- and they're easy to swap.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's sail is Indian orange -- a distinct term from international orange, which is used for the canopy exterior and seating of enclosed/open lifeboats. The two terms are easy to confuse since they name two different components.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-5.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 20 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What provides buoyancy in an open lifeboat, allowing it to remain afloat even when completely filled with water, equipment, and survivors?

choices.text: Buoyancy units -- usually air tanks or units of unicellular plastic foam
The wooden hull planking alone
choices.misconception: Attributes flotation to the hull material itself rather than dedicated buoyancy units.

choices.text: The engine compartment, sealed airtight for this purpose
choices.misconception: Invents an incorrect buoyancy source.

choices.text: Nothing specific -- open lifeboats simply can't be swamped
choices.misconception: Denies that swamping is possible and skips the actual feature that manages it.

hints: These are dedicated fittings built into the boat specifically for this purpose, not a side effect of the hull material.
questions.explanation: Buoyancy units -- usually air tanks or units of unicellular plastic foam -- keep an open lifeboat afloat even when completely filled with water, equipment, and survivors.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-3.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 9 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A liferaft's painter line normally parts on its own at a built-in weak link under excess strain. Why is a floating sheath knife also kept near the raft's entrance?

choices.text: As a backup -- in case the weak link fails to part, the line can be cut manually to free the raft
To repair tears in the canopy fabric
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated repair function for the knife.

choices.text: To open food ration packaging only
choices.misconception: Understates the knife's actual safety purpose.

choices.text: To signal rescuers by reflecting light off the blade
choices.misconception: Confuses a cutting tool with an unrelated signaling function.

hints: The weak link is meant to fail automatically, but a backup plan exists for when it doesn't.
The knife floats on purpose -- so it isn't lost overboard if dropped while cutting a line at the raft's entrance.
questions.explanation: A liferaft's painter is designed to part at a built-in weak link under excess strain, so the raft isn't dragged under with a sinking ship. The floating sheath knife kept near the entrance is a manual backup for exactly the case where the weak link fails to part -- the line can be cut by hand instead.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 17 'Weak link' and 23 'Floating Sheath Knife'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A liferaft's rigging includes both a 'weak link' and a towing bridle/connection. What's the key difference in how each is designed to behave under strain?

choices.text: The weak link is deliberately designed to part (break) under excess strain, so the raft isn't dragged under with a sinking ship; the towing bridle/connection is designed to hold under load, for actually towing the raft
Both are designed to hold under any amount of strain, with no intentional failure point anywhere in the system
choices.misconception: Misses that the weak link is intentionally the system's failure point -- that's its entire purpose.

choices.text: The towing bridle is the part designed to break; the weak link is what holds during towing
choices.misconception: Reverses the two components' actual roles.

choices.text: They're the same fitting under two different names
choices.misconception: Treats two distinct, separately labeled fittings as if they were one.

hints: One of these two fittings is supposed to fail on purpose -- the other is supposed to never fail.
Think about what would happen to the raft if the ship it's still attached to starts sinking fast.
questions.explanation: A weak link is deliberately built into a liferaft's painter/towing line so it parts under excess strain -- protecting the raft from being dragged under by a sinking ship. The towing bridle and towing connection, by contrast, are the load-bearing hardware meant to hold firmly while a raft is actually being towed by a rescue vessel.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 17 'Weak link', 19 'Towing Connection', and 20 'Towing bridle'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both a sea anchor and ballast bags. What's the difference in what each one does?

choices.text: The sea anchor (deployed on the towing/painter line) stabilizes the raft's drift and slows it; the ballast bags (hanging beneath the raft in the water) add stability and resist capsizing by weighting the raft's underside
Both serve the exact same anti-capsize function, just mounted in different places
choices.misconception: Collapses two distinct functions -- drift stabilization vs. anti-capsize weighting -- into one.

choices.text: The ballast bags stabilize drift, and the sea anchor resists capsizing
choices.misconception: Swaps the two fittings' actual functions.

choices.text: The sea anchor is a fishing tool, unrelated to raft stability
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated function for the sea anchor.

hints: One of these fittings is about controlling which way and how fast the raft drifts; the other is about keeping the raft from flipping over.
questions.explanation: A sea anchor (or drogue) is deployed on the raft's towing/painter line to stabilize its drift and slow unwanted movement across the water. Ballast bags are separate water pockets that hang beneath the raft, adding stability and resisting capsizing by weighting the raft's underside -- two different fittings solving two different problems.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 13 'Ballast bags' and 18 'Sea Anchor'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both a canopy and an inflatable floor. Both help protect survivors from the cold, but from which direction?

choices.text: The canopy protects from wind, spray, and heat loss from above and the sides; the inflatable floor insulates against cold transmitted from the water directly beneath the raft
Both protect against cold from the same direction -- overhead only
choices.misconception: Misses that the floor addresses a completely different exposure direction (from below) than the canopy (from above/the sides).

choices.text: The inflatable floor is only for buoyancy, not insulation
choices.misconception: Drops the floor's real insulating role against cold transmitted from the water below.

choices.text: Only the canopy matters for cold protection; the floor's shape is irrelevant to warmth
choices.misconception: Dismisses the floor's genuine insulating function.

hints: Cold can reach survivors from more than one direction in a liferaft -- wind and spray from the sides/above, and conducted cold from the water right underneath them.
questions.explanation: The canopy protects occupants from wind, spray, and heat loss from above and the sides, while the inflatable floor insulates against cold conducted up from the water directly beneath the raft -- two separate features addressing cold exposure from two different directions.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 6 'Canopy' and 22 'Inflatable floor'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both an internal lifeline and external lifelines. What's the difference?

choices.text: The internal lifeline runs inside the raft for occupants to hold onto; the external lifelines run around the outside for swimmers in the water to grab onto
They're the same line, just referred to differently depending on which side you're looking from
choices.misconception: Treats two separately labeled fittings, mounted in two different locations, as if they were one.

choices.text: The internal lifeline is for swimmers in the water; the external lifelines are for occupants inside the raft
choices.misconception: Swaps the two fittings' actual locations and purposes.

choices.text: Only the external lifelines actually exist on a liferaft; 'internal lifeline' just means the same fitting
choices.misconception: Denies that the raft has a genuinely separate interior fitting.

hints: One of these is for people already inside the raft; the other is for people still in the water.
questions.explanation: The internal lifeline runs inside the raft (across the canopy interior) for occupants to hold onto, while the external lifelines run around the outside of the raft for swimmers still in the water to grab onto.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 1 'Internal lifeline' and 12 'External lifelines'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both a recognition light and a water-activated battery. How do these two features relate to each other?

choices.text: The water-activated battery automatically powers the recognition light once the raft is in the water, without anyone needing to manually turn it on
They're unrelated -- the recognition light runs on hand-cranked power, not any battery
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated power source for the recognition light.

choices.text: The water-activated battery is only a backup for a separate EPIRB, unrelated to the recognition light
choices.misconception: Invents a connection to unrelated equipment instead of the actual recognition-light link.

choices.text: The recognition light must be switched on manually, and the water-activated battery only powers the CO2 inflation system
choices.misconception: Misassigns what the water-activated battery actually powers, and drops the light's automatic activation.

hints: Think about why a light on a liferaft would need to work automatically, in the dark, without anyone fumbling for a switch.
questions.explanation: A liferaft's water-activated battery automatically powers its recognition light as soon as the raft is in the water -- no manual switch required, which matters since survivors may board in darkness or under stress.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 3 'Recognition light' and 7 'Water activated battery'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch
